title: Trust-Repair Mechanics

content.statement: Trust can be rebuilt, but only with speed and sincerity.
content.question: When your business messes up, what's the repair sequence? Is it written down?
content.equation: TRM = Acknowledgment × Speed × Compensation Fit × Follow-Up
sop: Write a standard failure-response sequence in advance: acknowledge, apologize, compensate, follow up, before you need it.
Set an internal target for how fast a service failure gets a human response, for example under 2 hours.
Rate your last real recovery on Acknowledgment, Speed, Compensation Fit, and Follow-Up, each 1-10 (see calculation, page 5).
Match your compensation to what was actually broken, a refund doesn't fix a missed deadline that cost someone else money.
Follow up 1-2 weeks later to confirm the relationship actually recovered, not just that the complaint closed.
content.explainer: Failure + excellent recovery = deeper trust than never having failed. Plan the failure response in advance.
questions: When your business messes up, what's the repair sequence? Is it written down?
Think of the last time you failed a customer, did your response make the relationship stronger or just end the complaint?
What's your compensation default when you're wrong, and does it actually fit what you broke?
content.equation_example: Rate each 1-10: Acknowledgment (how fast and genuinely you owned it) 9, Speed (response time) 8, Compensation Fit (how well the fix matched the harm) 7, Follow-Up (did you check back) 6. Score = 9 times 8 times 7 times 6 = 3,024. Higher means the recovery likely deepened trust; any single low number drags the whole score down and means the repair probably didn't stick.
content.closing_statement: How you fail is a decision, not an accident.

caption:
Principle Nº 89: Trust-Repair Mechanics

Trust can be rebuilt, but only with speed and sincerity. Failure + excellent recovery = deeper trust than never having failed.

Ask yourself: When your business messes up, what's the repair sequence? Is it written down?
